draft.introduction: The Ordinatio of John Duns Scotus (c. 1300) is his commentary on Lombard’s Sentences and his principal theological work. Scotus’s “subtle” distinctions—especially the univocity of being and the formal distinction—reshaped late medieval philosophy.

translation.text: Whether it is necessary for a human being in the present state of life to be supernaturally inspired with some special knowledge. I argue that it is: for the sufficient knowledge of the end to which a human being is ordered exceeds the natural capacity of the intellect in the present state. Therefore some supernatural knowledge must be given to the wayfarer.
translation.original_text: [Latin: Utrum homini pro statu isto sit necessarium aliquam doctrinam specialem supernaturaliter inspirari]

translator_notes.note: Scotus’s argument for the necessity of revelation begins from a different premise than Aquinas. While Aquinas argues that revelation is needed because some truths exceed reason, Scotus argues that humans are ordered toward a supernatural end (the beatific vision) that natural reason could never discover. This shifts the emphasis from reason’s limits to the will’s infinite capacity for God.
